context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: The Question of Ethics

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Development Planners are often faced with having to compromise in reconciling demands from very different groups, and are exposed to (sometimes conflicting) sets of responsibility (e.g. personal, professional, authority). Within this context, what is an ethical compromise? What is the ethics of planners remaining "neutral"? What responsibility should influence their practice? What is the source of ethical values? What ethical framework governs a context of radical cultural pluralism, as in a Western planner working in non-Western countries?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help with a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. I’m a little stuck on the ethics prompt, especially how to talk about “ethical compromise” and whether planners can really be neutral.
exchanges.tutor: Great, let's dig in! Before we talk about structure or arguments, let me ask you this: in your own words, what do you think makes a compromise *ethical* rather than just... a compromise? What's the difference, in your mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting, so I should orient them to the prompt without giving away any direction or framing. A good opening Socratic move is to ask them what they already think about one of the core tensions in the prompt, rather than explaining anything. I'll pick the most fundamental question — what an ethical compromise even means — to get them thinking from their own perspective first.

exchanges.student: I think an ethical compromise is when I’m not just giving in for convenience or politics, but balancing harms and responsibilities in a way I can still justify. Maybe the difference is whether it still protects people’s dignity or basic rights, even if nobody gets exactly what they want.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point — the idea of justifiability is important. But here's a challenge for you: who gets to define what 'dignity' or 'basic rights' means? Does that definition stay the same when a Western planner is working in a non-Western context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a thoughtful initial answer that touches on harm-balancing, justifiability, dignity, and rights. This is good raw material. Rather than validating and moving on too quickly, I should push them to think deeper — specifically, who decides what counts as 'dignity' or 'basic rights' in a cross-cultural planning context? This connects directly to the prompt's question about radical cultural pluralism and will help them see the complexity ahead.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s where I get stuck. My first instinct is to say human rights should apply everywhere, but that also feels too easy because it can turn into outsiders imposing their own values. I’m not sure how to say that without sounding like “anything goes” culturally.
exchanges.tutor: You've put your finger on a real dilemma — and notice you haven't fallen into either trap yet. So let me push you further: if a planner can't fully rely on universal values *or* just defer to local norms, what does that leave them with? Where does their ethical grounding actually come from in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuine tension between universalism and cultural relativism, which is central to the prompt. They're worried about falling into relativism but also don't want to sound naively universalist. This is productive discomfort. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to think about what a planner actually *does* in that tension — not just philosophically, but practically. This will help them connect abstract ethics to the planner's role, which is what the prompt is really asking about.

exchanges.student: Maybe from a mix of sources? Like professional ethics, local participation, awareness of power, and some baseline commitment to not causing serious harm. I’m just not sure if that’s too vague, or if I need one clearer framework instead of a mix.
exchanges.tutor: Interesting that you're looking for one clean framework — why do you think that would be more satisfying? What does the need for a single framework assume about ethics itself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is questioning whether a pluralistic ethical framework is rigorous enough, which shows good critical thinking. Rather than resolving this tension for them, I should push them to think about what the prompt itself implies — it asks about 'radical cultural pluralism,' suggesting that a single clean framework may itself be part of the problem. I want them to interrogate their instinct that one framework is necessarily better, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess I want one framework because it feels safer and easier to defend. But maybe that assumes ethics is supposed to be neat and universal, when in this class the whole point seems to be that planning happens in messy power relations and conflicting obligations.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful shift in your thinking. Now connect it to the prompt's other question: if ethics is inherently messy and context-bound, what does that say about a planner claiming to be 'neutral'? Is neutrality even possible — and is it ethical?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a significant conceptual leap — connecting their desire for a clean framework to an assumption about universality, and then linking that to the course's broader themes of power and complexity. This is excellent progress. I should affirm the direction without over-praising, and now push them toward the neutrality question, which they flagged at the start as a sticking point and which is explicitly in the prompt. This keeps the conversation moving forward.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking neutrality probably isn’t really possible, because planners always work through institutions, laws, funding, and power. Even choosing not to take a position helps the status quo, so calling that “neutral” can hide political and ethical choices. Maybe the more ethical move is being transparent about my position and accountable for its effects, but I’m not fully sure.
exchanges.tutor: You've built up a lot of solid thinking across our conversation — on ethical compromise, cultural pluralism, the limits of universal frameworks, and now the myth of neutrality. I think you have enough to start writing. What would your opening argument be if you had to state it in one or two sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong and substantive argument about neutrality as a political fiction, connecting it to institutional power and accountability. This is very good and covers significant ground. We've now touched on most of the prompt's key questions across 5 exchanges. To avoid redundancy and move toward the writing, I should push them to integrate what they've developed so far into a written attempt. This is a natural moment to ask them to synthesize.

exchanges.student: Maybe: ethical compromise in development planning is not value-free bargaining, but a justified negotiation among conflicting responsibilities that must still protect people from serious harm and domination. Because planning is always shaped by power, neutrality is mostly a myth, so the planner’s real ethical duty is reflexive, transparent, and accountable practice rather than pretending to stand outside politics.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong opening argument with real substance. But look back at the prompt — there are two questions you haven't fully addressed yet. What responsibilities should influence a planner's practice, and specifically what happens to your framework when a Western planner works in a non-Western context? How does your argument hold up there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong thesis-level statement that integrates several key ideas from our discussion. This is good work. Rather than over-praising or critiquing it extensively, I should push them to think about what's still missing from the prompt — specifically the cross-cultural/Western-non-Western dimension and the question of where ethical values come from. These are explicit parts of the prompt they haven't fully addressed yet, and they need to be in the reflection.

exchanges.student: I think the responsibilities part includes professional duty, responsibility to affected communities, and maybe a broader responsibility to justice, not just to employers or the state. For the Western planner part, I’d say my framework still works, but it gets stricter: I’d have to be more self-critical about power, colonial legacies, and whether “help” is actually overriding local agency.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've now touched on all the major parts of the prompt. One thing to watch as you write: your point about colonial legacies and local agency is important but underdeveloped so far. Make sure it gets real weight in your reflection, not just a mention. Now go write a first draft — what's your first paragraph going to focus on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed all major components of the prompt across our conversation. They've covered ethical compromise, sources of ethical values, neutrality, cultural pluralism, and the Western planner in non-Western contexts. This is the 8th exchange and we're approaching the end of the planned conversation length. It's time to push them firmly toward drafting. I should briefly note what's strong and what to watch out for in the writing, without giving them language or structure to copy.

exchanges.student: I think my first paragraph will define ethical compromise and argue that planning always involves conflicting duties, so ethics is about how those tradeoffs are justified. Then I’d connect that right away to why neutrality is misleading, because planners are already inside power structures.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a focused and logical opening. You have everything you need to draft now. Remember to give real depth to the colonial legacies point — don't let it stay abstract. Go write it, and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan for their opening and it's well-structured. We're near the end of the planned conversation length and the student has enough to write a solid draft. I should encourage them to start writing and remind them that the colonial legacies/local agency thread and the source of ethical values need sufficient development in the body of the piece. I should not give them more scaffolding — they need to write now.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll try drafting it that way. If I get stuck, I’ll probably need help making the cultural pluralism/colonialism part more concrete instead of just sounding generic.
exchanges.tutor: Good self-awareness. When you come back, bring what you've written — I won't help you develop that section in the abstract. The concreteness you're looking for will come from engaging with your own draft first. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and flagging a potential future sticking point. This is appropriate self-awareness. I should affirm that and remind them that when they come back, they'll need to bring a written attempt — I won't just help them theorize further without seeing their draft. This sets clear expectations and keeps them accountable to actually writing.
